items.title: Understanding Gonorrhea Transmission and Epidemiology
items.content: Gonorrhea is a sexually transmitted infection with significant public health implications, particularly due to its high transmission rate from males to females and the role of asymptomatic carriers in sustaining infection rates.
keyPoints: Transmission rate to women during unprotected sex with an infected man is ~50–80%.
Oropharyngeal gonorrhea occurs in ~20% of women practicing fellatio with infected partners.
Asymptomatic individuals play a crucial role in sustaining infection rates by continuing sexual activity without treatment.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · Gonococcal Infections | DEFINITION | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Epidemiological Trends and Public Health Concerns of Gonorrhea
items.content: The incidence of gonorrhea has fluctuated over the years, with recent increases in reported cases and rising antibiotic resistance posing significant public health challenges.
keyPoints: In 2021, ~710,000 new cases were reported in the U.S., a 136% increase since 2009.
Gonorrhea remains a major global health issue with an estimated 82.4 million cases worldwide among adults aged 15 to 49 years in 2020.
Antibiotic-resistant N. gonorrhoeae is one of the three most urgent threats identified by the CDC.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · Gonococcal Infections | DEFINITION | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Demographic and Behavioral Risk Factors for Gonorrhea
items.content: Certain demographic groups are at higher risk for gonorrhea, influenced by factors such as age, ethnicity, and sexual behavior.
keyPoints: Highest attack rates in the U.S. are among 15- to 24-year-old women (730.5 per 100,000) and 20- to 29-year-old men (813.6 per 100,000).
>70% of reported cases occur in these age groups.
Rates are highest among African Americans (652.9 per 100,000) and lowest among persons of Asian descent (37.8 per 100,000).
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · Gonococcal Infections | DEFINITION | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Pili and Gonococcal Pathogenicity
items.content: Pili are crucial for N. gonorrhoeae adherence to human mucosal surfaces, enhancing virulence by facilitating attachment, invasion, and transport through epithelial cells.
keyPoints: Piliated strains adhere better to human mucosal cells than nonpiliated variants.
Pili mediate attachment to nonciliated columnar epithelial cells in fallopian tube models.
Pilus expression is rapidly switched off with unselected subculture due to gene rearrangements, leading to antigenic variation.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.title: Opacity-Associated Protein and Immune Evasion
items.content: Opa proteins facilitate gonococcal adherence to epithelial cells and immune evasion by binding CEACAM receptors, suppressing T lymphocyte activation and promoting non-opsonic phagocytosis.
keyPoints: Opa contributes to inter-gonococcal adhesion and attachment to eukaryotic cells including PMNs.
Certain Opa variants promote epithelial cell invasion by binding vitronectin, heparan sulfate proteoglycans, and CEACAM receptors.
CEACAM-binding gonococci suppress CD4+ T lymphocyte activation and proliferation.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.title: Porin's Role in Gonococcal Serotyping and Immune Evasion
items.content: Porin exhibits antigenic variation crucial for serotyping, with PorB.1A strains linked to disseminated gonococcal infection due to resistance to serum killing and diminished inflammatory response.
keyPoints: Porin is the most abundant surface protein, forming anion-transporting channels in the outer membrane.
Two main porin serotypes: PorB.1A (associated with DGI) and PorB.1B (causes local genital infections).
PorB.1A strains resist serum killing and inhibit inflammatory response by binding complement-inhibitory molecules.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.title: Gonococcal LOS and Immune Evasion
items.content: Gonococcal lipooligosaccharide (LOS) undergoes phase variation affecting its interaction with the host immune system. Sialylation of LOS inhibits complement pathways, reduces neutrophil binding, and decreases oxidative burst in PMNs. The terminal monosaccharide structure of LOS determines dendritic cell receptor engagement, influencing T-helper responses.
keyPoints: LOS sialylation inhibits classic and alternative complement pathways by reducing IgG binding.
Sialylated LOS reduces nonopsonic Opa-mediated association with neutrophils.
Terminal monosaccharide of LOS dictates C-type lectin receptor engagement on dendritic cells, influencing T-helper responses.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.title: Host Factors in Gonococcal Infection
items.content: Gonococci utilize host factors to invade epithelial cells. Activation of phosphatidylcholine-specific phospholipase C and acidic sphingomyelinase by N. gonorrhoeae releases diacylglycerol and ceramide, leading to apoptosis and disrupted epithelial integrity. Complement activation results in inflammation through chemotactic factor release.
keyPoints: Gonococcal entry into epithelial cells requires activation of phosphatidylcholine-specific phospholipase C and acidic sphingomyelinase.
Ceramide accumulation leads to apoptosis, disrupting epithelial integrity.
Complement activation releases chemotactic factors contributing to inflammation.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.title: Gonococcal Antimicrobial Resistance
items.content: N. gonorrhoeae has developed resistance to multiple antibiotics due to its ability to alter antigenic structures. Sulfonamides became ineffective within a decade of introduction, and by 1965, 42% of isolates showed low-level penicillin G resistance, later escalating with penicillinase production.
keyPoints: Sulfonamides were ineffective against gonorrhea within a decade.
By 1965, 42% of gonococcal isolates had low-level resistance to penicillin G.
Penicillinase production contributed to increased resistance.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.title: Mechanisms of Antibiotic Resistance in N. gonorrhoeae
items.content: N. gonorrhoeae develops resistance through chromosomal mutations and acquisition of R factors. Chromosomal mutations can be single-step or involve multiple loci, leading to high-level resistance. Plasmid-mediated resistance includes beta-lactamase production and tetracycline resistance.
keyPoints: Chromosomal mutations account for ~16% resistance to penicillin/tetracycline in the U.S.
Beta-lactamase plasmids spread by early 1980s, leading to PPNG
Plasmid-borne tetracycline resistance (TRNG) can coexist with chromosomal mutations
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 3)

items.title: Emergence and Spread of Quinolone Resistance in N. gonorrhoeae
items.content: Quinolone resistance emerged soon after their use for treating gonorrhea, with high prevalence in the Pacific Islands, Asia, Europe, and the Middle East. DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV alterations are implicated.
keyPoints: QRNG is prevalent in Pacific Islands, Asia, parts of Europe, and the Middle East
In the U.S., QRNG identified predominantly on the Pacific coast
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 3)

items.title: Concerns Regarding Cephalosporin Resistance in N. gonorrhoeae
items.content: Ceftriaxone resistance is rising with MICs reaching up to 2 μg/mL. Mutations in penA, mtrR, and penB genes contribute to this resistance. Cefixime's removal from first-line treatment is due to its limited efficacy.
keyPoints: Ceftriaxone MICs can reach 0.015–0.125 μg/mL
Mutations in penA (60-70 amino acids difference), mtrR, and penB contribute to resistance
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 3)

items.title: Gonococcal Urethritis: Incubation and Symptomatology
items.content: In men, gonococcal urethritis typically presents with an incubation period of 2–7 days. The disease is characterized by urethral discharge and dysuria without urinary frequency or urgency. Strains such as PorB.1A are associated with mild and asymptomatic cases.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 2–7 days
Symptoms: Urethral discharge, dysuria
PorB.1A strains cause more mild/asymptomatic urethritis

items.title: Gonococcal Infections in Women: Cervicitis Presentation and Complications
items.content: In women, mucopurulent cervicitis is a common manifestation of gonorrhea. Symptoms may include vaginal discharge and dysuria. Speculum examination often reveals mucopurulent discharge or an inflamed cervix. Gonococcal infection can extend to cause pelvic inflammatory disease (PID).
keyPoints: Symptoms: Vaginal discharge, dysuria
Speculum findings: Mucopurulent discharge, inflamed cervix
Complications: Pelvic inflammatory disease

items.title: Asymptomatic Gonococcal Infections in Men and Women
items.content: A significant reservoir of gonococcal infections exists among asymptomatic individuals. In men, these asymptomatic cases contribute to the spread of infection. In women, about half remain asymptomatic or have minor symptoms, delaying medical consultation.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic reservoir in both genders
Men: Major source of infection spread
Women: 50% may be asymptomatic

items.title: Gonococcal Vaginitis in Anestrogenic Women
items.content: Gonococcal vaginitis is rare in healthy women but can occur in anestrogenic women due to the thinning of vaginal epithelium, leading to intense inflammation and pain during examination.
keyPoints: Occurs in prepubertal girls and postmenopausal women with thinned vaginal epithelium
Symptoms include red, edematous mucosa and abundant purulent discharge
Often accompanied by infection in the urethra and Skene's/Bartolin's glands

items.title: Anorectal Gonorrhea Prevalence and Resistance
items.content: Anorectal gonorrhea is primarily seen in MSM, with a significant increase in resistance to antimicrobial agents due to specific genetic mutations.
keyPoints: Rectum is the sole site of infection in 5% of women with gonorrhea
Resurgence among MSM since the 1990s, rates more than doubled in recent years
Increased resistance due to mtrR mutation or promoter region changes

items.title: Ocular Gonorrhea: Recognition and Treatment
items.content: Ocular gonorrhea, often resulting from autoinoculation, requires prompt recognition and treatment to prevent severe complications.
keyPoints: Results from autoinoculation from an infected genital site
Symptoms include swollen eyelid, hyperemia, chemosis, and purulent discharge
Diagnosis confirmed by Gram stain and culture of the discharge

items.title: Ophthalmia Neonatorum: Prophylaxis and Treatment
items.content: Neonatal gonococcal ophthalmia is prevented by prophylactic agents like 0.5% erythromycin ointment, but requires systemic antibiotics for treatment.
keyPoints: Ophthalmia neonatorum occurs in neonates exposed to infected cervical secretions during birth.
Prophylaxis with 0.5% erythromycin ophthalmic ointment prevents the condition.
Treatment necessitates systemic antibiotics, not just prophylactic eye drops or ointments.

items.title: Disseminated Gonococcal Infection (DGI) in Newborns
items.content: In newborns, DGI often presents as septic arthritis and may lead to rare complications like meningitis or pneumonia.
keyPoints: Septic arthritis is the most common manifestation of systemic gonococcal infection in newborns.
DGI onset typically occurs between 3–21 days of age with polyarticular involvement being common.
Rare instances include sepsis, meningitis, and pneumonia.

items.title: Disseminated Gonococcal Infection: Clinical Stages and Risk Factors
items.content: DGI is characterized by a bacteremic stage with systemic symptoms and a joint-localized stage; menstruation increases risk in women.
keyPoints: DGI has two stages: bacteremic (less common today) and joint-localized with suppurative arthritis.
The bacteremic stage features higher temperatures, chills, painful joints, tenosynovitis, and skin lesions (~75% of patients).
Menstruation is a risk factor for DGI dissemination in women, with symptoms often starting within 7 days post-menses.

items.title: Gonorrhea Enhances HIV Transmission and Acquisition
items.content: In HIV-infected persons, gonococcal infections significantly increase the risk of both transmitting and acquiring HIV. This is due to increased viral shedding in individuals with urethritis or cervicitis and a higher number of target cells for HIV infection.
keyPoints: Gonorrhea enhances HIV transmission three- to fivefold.
HIV detected more commonly by PCR in ejaculates from men with gonococcal urethritis than nongonococcal urethritis.
PCR positivity diminishes twofold after appropriate therapy for urethritis.
Greater number of CD4+ T lymphocytes and dendritic cells available for HIV infection in endocervical secretions from women with nonulcerative STIs compared to those with ulcerative STIs.

items.title: Impact of Gonorrhea on HIV Viral Shedding
items.content: Gonococcal infections in HIV-positive men lead to increased detection of HIV by PCR in ejaculates, suggesting enhanced viral shedding. Effective treatment for urethritis reduces this PCR positivity.
keyPoints: HIV detected more commonly by PCR in ejaculates from HIV-positive men with gonococcal urethritis than nongonococcal urethritis.
PCR positivity diminishes twofold after appropriate therapy for urethritis.

items.title: Diagnosis of Gonococcal Urethritis in Men
items.content: Rapid diagnosis of gonococcal urethritis can be achieved through Gram staining of urethral exudates, which is highly specific and sensitive for symptomatic males.
keyPoints: Gram-negative intracellular monococci and diplococci are detected with high specificity and sensitivity in diagnosing gonococcal urethritis in men.
~50% sensitivity when diagnosing gonococcal cervicitis using the same method.

items.title: Use of NAATs for Gonorrhea Diagnosis
items.content: NAATs are sensitive diagnostic tests for gonorrhea, capable of detecting both N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis from urine samples with high sensitivity.
keyPoints: NAATs include Roche COBAS AMPLICOR, Gen-Probe Aptima Combo 2, Cepheid Xpert® CT/NG Assay, and BD ProbeTec ET.
Urine samples tested by NAATs have similar or greater sensitivity compared to urethral or cervical swab samples assessed by other methods.

items.title: Diagnosis of Gonococcal Infection in Children
items.content: Standardized culture is the preferred method for diagnosing gonococcal infection in children, with two positive NAATs as an alternative legal evidence.
keyPoints: Cultures should be obtained from pharynx and anus of both genders, urethra of boys, and vagina of girls; cervical specimens are not recommended for prepubertal girls.
Two positive NAATs targeting different nucleic acid sequences may substitute culture as legal evidence in children.

items.title: First-Line Treatment for Uncomplicated Gonorrhea
items.content: The CDC recommends ceftriaxone as the first-line treatment for uncomplicated gonococcal infections, administered at a doubled dose of 500 mg IM due to increased MICs. This regimen is effective in treating urethral, cervical, rectal, and pharyngeal infections.
keyPoints: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose recommended for uncomplicated gonorrhea.
Dose increase based on doubling of mean inhibitory concentrations (MICs) compared to 20 years ago.
Effective cure rate with ceftriaxone is almost always achieved.

items.title: Management of Co-infection and Resistance
items.content: Concurrent treatment with doxycycline is recommended for chlamydial infection when it cannot be excluded, due to frequent co-infection. Azithromycin resistance in N. gonorrhoeae has increased significantly, leading to its removal from first-line regimens.
keyPoints: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days recommended if chlamydial infection cannot be excluded.
Azithromycin resistance increased more than sevenfold to 4.7% in 2021.
Azithromycin no longer part of first-line regimen due to resistance.

items.title: Alternative Regimens and Hospitalization Indications
items.content: Gentamicin plus azithromycin is recommended as an alternative treatment for uncomplicated gonorrhea. Hospitalization may be necessary if the diagnosis is uncertain, in cases of suppurative arthritis, or when patient adherence to outpatient treatment cannot be ensured.
keyPoints: Gentamicin (240 mg IM single dose) plus azithromycin (2 g oral single dose) as alternative regimen.
Hospitalization indicated for uncertain diagnosis, suppurative arthritis, or poor adherence.
Spectinomycin not available in the U.S. and limited effectiveness for pharyngeal infections.

items.title: Test of Cure for Gonococcal Pharyngeal Infection
items.content: Persons with gonococcal pharyngeal infection should undergo a test of cure 7–14 days after treatment to ensure eradication or detect possible treatment failure, regardless of the regimen used.
keyPoints: Test of cure is mandatory for pharyngeal infections due to difficulty in eradication compared to genital infections.
Perform the test of cure 7–14 days post-treatment.
Spectinomycin has a cure rate of ≤52%, necessitating early culture (3–5 days) post-treatment if used.

items.title: Management and Follow-up for Gonococcal Infection
items.content: For gonococcal infections, cultures should be performed if symptoms persist post-treatment. Retesting is recommended within 3 months due to high reinfection rates.
keyPoints: Cultures are necessary if symptoms persist after treatment with an established regimen.
Retest for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis 3 months post-treatment due to high reinfection rates (6–12 months).
Spectinomycin-treated patients should have a pharyngeal sample cultured early as a test of cure.

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of Neisseria gonorrhoeae?
options: It grows in chains and clusters.
It contains, on average, three genome copies per coccal unit.
It can utilize maltose as a carbon source.
items.explanation: Neisseria gonorrhoeae is polyploid with an average of three genome copies per coccal unit, allowing for antigenic variation and survival in its host.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · Gonococcal Infections | DEFINITION | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the primary mode of transmission for gonorrhea?
options: From males to females during unprotected sexual intercourse.
items.explanation: Gonorrhea is transmitted more efficiently from males to females during unprotected sexual encounters, with a transmission rate of ~50–80% per encounter.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · Gonococcal Infections | DEFINITION | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which group has the highest reported incidence of gonorrhea in the United States?
items.explanation: The highest attack rate in the United States is among 20- to 29-year-old men, with 813.6 per 100,000 population reported cases.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · Gonococcal Infections | DEFINITION | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a complication of untreated gonorrhea in female patients?
items.explanation: Untreated gonococcal infections can lead to perihepatitis among other complications in female patients.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · Gonococcal Infections | DEFINITION | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which gonococcal surface protein is essential for genetic competence and transformation of N. gonorrhoeae?
items.explanation: Pili are essential for the genetic competence and transformation of N. gonorrhoeae, allowing horizontal transfer of genetic material.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of opacity-associated protein (Opa) in N. gonorrhoeae?
options: It mediates attachment to nonciliated columnar epithelial cells.
It contributes to inter-gonococcal adhesion and adherence to eukaryotic cells.
It is the most abundant gonococcal surface protein.
items.explanation: Opa contributes to inter-gonococcal adhesion, responsible for the opaque nature of colonies and adherence to various eukaryotic cells.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.question: Which porin serotype is often associated with disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI)?
items.explanation: PorB.1A strains are often associated with disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI).
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.question: What is the function of IgA1 protease produced by N. gonorrhoeae?
options: It enhances attachment to human endometrial cells.
It scavenges iron from transferrin and lactoferrin.
It protects the organism from mucosal IgA action.
items.explanation: IgA1 protease may protect N. gonorrhoeae from the action of mucosal IgA.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.question: Which component of gonococcal lipooligosaccharide (LOS) is involved in inhibiting the classic pathway of complement?
items.explanation: Sialylation at LOS sites inhibits the classic pathway of complement.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.question: What is a consequence of ceramide accumulation within epithelial cells during gonococcal infection?
options: Apoptosis leading to disrupted epithelial integrity
Activation of the alternative pathway of complement
items.explanation: Ceramide accumulation within cells leads to apoptosis, disrupting epithelial integrity.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.question: Which type of immune response is potentially less favorable for the clearance of gonococcal infection?
items.explanation: A T_H2-type immune response may be less favorable for clearance of gonococcal infection.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.question: Which outer-membrane protein in N. gonorrhoeae shows little interstrain antigenic variation and can block the effect of bactericidal antibodies to porin and LOS?
items.explanation: Rmp shows little interstrain antigenic variation and can block bactericidal antibodies to porin and LOS.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.question: Which mechanism is primarily responsible for high-level resistance to penicillin in N. gonorrhoeae?
items.explanation: $\beta$-Lactamase (penicillinase)-producing strains of N. gonorrhoeae carrying $\beta$-lactamase plasmids were responsible for high-level penicillin resistance.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 3)

items.question: Which antibiotic is no longer reliable for the treatment of gonorrhea due to widespread resistance?
items.explanation: Penicillin, along with ampicillin and tetracycline, is no longer reliable for treating gonorrhea due to resistance.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 3)

items.question: What mechanism contributes to cephalosporin resistance in N. gonorrhoeae?
items.explanation: Cephalosporin resistance involves mutations in the penA allele, which encodes a modified penicillin-binding protein.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 3)

items.question: Which antibiotic is recommended as an alternative for treating urogenital gonorrhea in China?
items.explanation: In China, spectinomycin is recommended as an alternative agent for primary treatment of urogenital gonorrhea.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 3)

items.question: What is the most common clinical manifestation of gonorrhea in male patients?
items.explanation: Acute urethritis is the most common clinical manifestation of gonorrhea in male patients.

items.question: Which serotype of Neisseria gonorrhoeae tends to cause a greater proportion of mild and asymptomatic urethritis?
items.explanation: Strains of the PorB.1A serotype tend to cause a greater proportion of cases of mild and asymptomatic urethritis.

items.question: What is the usual incubation period for gonorrhea after exposure in men?
items.explanation: The usual incubation period after exposure is 2–7 days before symptoms develop.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a major symptom of gonococcal urethritis in men?
items.explanation: When they occur, urethral discharge and dysuria usually do not include urinary frequency.

items.question: In which population is gonococcal vaginitis more likely to occur due to the thinning of vaginal stratified squamous epithelium?
items.explanation: Gonococcal vaginitis is more likely in anestrogenic women, such as postmenopausal and prepubertal girls, where the vaginal epithelium can be thinned.

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is NOT typically associated with anorectal gonorrhea in women?
items.explanation: Severe hyperemia and chemosis are symptoms of ocular gonorrhea, not anorectal gonorrhea.

items.question: What is a common mode of transmission for pharyngeal gonorrhea?
options: Vertical transmission from mother to child
items.explanation: Pharyngeal gonorrhea is usually transmitted through oral-genital sexual exposure, particularly fellatio.

items.question: What is a potential complication of untreated maternal gonococcal infection at term?
items.explanation: Untreated maternal gonococcal infection can lead to complications such as chorioamnionitis.

items.question: Which prophylactic agent is effective in preventing ophthalmia neonatorum but not for its treatment?
items.explanation: Ocular neonatal instillation of 0.5% erythromycin ophthalmic ointment prevents ophthalmia neonatorum but is not effective for its treatment.

items.question: What is the most common manifestation of systemic infection in newborns with gonorrhea?
items.explanation: The most common manifestation of systemic infection or disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI) in the newborn is septic arthritis.

items.question: Which joints are most commonly involved in suppurative gonococcal arthritis?
items.explanation: Suppurative gonococcal arthritis most often involves the knees, wrists, ankles, and elbows.

items.question: What is a risk factor for dissemination of gonococcal infection in women?
items.explanation: Menstruation is a risk factor for dissemination, and approximately two-thirds of cases of DGI are in women.

items.question: Which sexually transmitted infection has been shown to enhance the transmission of HIV three- to fivefold?
items.explanation: Nonulcerative STIs like gonorrhea enhance HIV transmission three- to fivefold.

items.question: In which group is HIV detected more commonly by PCR in ejaculates?
items.explanation: HIV is detected more commonly in ejaculates from HIV-positive men with gonococcal urethritis.

items.question: What happens to PCR positivity after appropriate therapy for urethritis?
items.explanation: PCR positivity diminishes twofold after appropriate therapy for urethritis.

items.question: Which type of swab should be used for collecting samples to diagnose gonococcal infections?
items.explanation: Samples should be collected with Dacron or rayon swabs.

items.question: What is the sensitivity of a single endocervical culture for diagnosing gonorrhea?
items.explanation: The sensitivity of a single endocervical culture is ~80–90%.

items.question: Why should specimens for gonococcal cultures be processed immediately?
options: To prevent contamination with other bacteria
Because gonococci do not tolerate drying
To enhance the growth of gonococci on selective media
items.explanation: It is important to process all samples immediately because gonococci do not tolerate drying.

items.question: Which of the following statements about NAATs for gonorrhea and chlamydia is correct?
options: They cannot be used with urine samples
They are less sensitive than culture methods
They can detect both N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis
They require a pelvic exam to collect samples
items.explanation: NAATs also detect C. trachomatis and are more sensitive than culture for identification of either N. gonorrhoeae or C. trachomatis.

items.question: Which antibiotic is recommended as the first-line treatment for uncomplicated gonococcal infections according to CDC guidelines?
items.explanation: The third-generation cephalosporin ceftriaxone is recommended as the first-line regimen for uncomplicated gonococcal infections.

items.question: What should be done if treatment failure occurs with a recommended regimen?
options: Switch to an alternative antibiotic immediately
Evaluate for reinfection or drug-resistant strain
Continue the same treatment for another week
items.explanation: True failure of treatment should prompt evaluation for reinfection, infection with a drug-resistant strain, or an alternative diagnosis.

items.question: Why is azithromycin no longer recommended as part of the first-line regimen for gonorrhea?
options: Resistance to azithromycin has increased significantly
It is ineffective against N. gonorrhoeae
It is only effective for chlamydial infection
items.explanation: Resistance to azithromycin of U.S. isolates of N. gonorrhoeae has increased more than sevenfold, leading to its discontinuation as part of the first-line regimen.

items.question: Which antibiotic is recommended for concurrent treatment with ceftriaxone if chlamydial infection cannot be excluded?
items.explanation: Concurrent treatment with doxycycline is recommended if chlamydial infection cannot be excluded.

items.question: Which statement is true regarding the treatment of gonococcal pharyngeal infections?
options: A test of cure is not necessary if symptoms resolve after therapy.
Persons with pharyngeal infection should undergo a test of cure regardless of the treatment regimen, 7–14 days after treatment.
Spectinomycin guarantees eradication of gonococcal pharyngitis.
Treatment failure can be ignored if initial symptoms improve.
items.explanation: A test of cure is recommended for pharyngeal infections to ensure eradication or detect treatment failure.

items.question: What should be done if a patient treated with spectinomycin for gonococcal infection still has symptoms?
options: No further action is needed as the cure rate is high.
A subsequent pharyngeal sample should be cultured early (3–5 days) following treatment.
The patient should immediately receive a higher dose of spectinomycin.
Switch to an alternative regimen without testing.
items.explanation: A subsequent pharyngeal culture is recommended early after treatment with spectinomycin due to its lower cure rate.

items.question: When should persons previously treated for gonorrhea be retested?
options: 3 months after treatment due to high rates of reinfection.
Only if symptoms recur within a year.
items.explanation: Retesting is advised 3 months after treatment because of high reinfection rates.

items.statement: Neisseria gonorrhoeae can grow on selective media and utilize glucose but not maltose.
items.explanation: Gonococci are distinguished from other Neisseria species by their ability to use glucose but not maltose, sucrose, or lactose.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · Gonococcal Infections | DEFINITION | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: The incidence of gonorrhea has been consistently decreasing worldwide since 2009.
items.explanation: In the United States, there was a significant increase in reported cases by 2021, up 136% from their historic low in 2009.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · Gonococcal Infections | DEFINITION | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Piliated strains of N. gonorrhoeae are less virulent in organ culture models than nonpiliated variants.
items.explanation: Piliated strains adhere better to cells and are more virulent in organ culture models.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.statement: Porin molecules exist as monomers that provide anion-transporting channels.
items.explanation: Porin molecules exist as trimers providing anion-transporting aqueous channels.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.statement: Gonococcal LOS sialylation increases the binding of IgG and enhances the classic pathway of complement.
items.explanation: Sialylation at LOS sites inhibits the classic pathway of complement by reducing IgG binding.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.statement: Persons deficient in terminal complement components are predisposed to recurrent gonococcal infections.
items.explanation: Deficiency in terminal complement components (C5 through C9) leads to a predisposition for recurrent bacteremic gonococcal infections.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.statement: Quinolone-resistant N. gonorrhoeae is predominantly found in the Pacific Islands and Asia.
items.explanation: QRNG is particularly common in the Pacific Islands, including Hawaii, and Asia.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 3)

items.statement: Ceftriaxone resistance in N. gonorrhoeae is due to alterations in DNA gyrase.
items.explanation: Cephalosporin resistance involves mutations in the penA allele, not alterations in DNA gyrase.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 3)

items.statement: Most cases of urethritis in the United States today are caused by N. gonorrhoeae or C. trachomatis.
items.explanation: The majority of cases of urethritis seen in the United States today are not caused by N. gonorrhoeae and/or C. trachomatis.

items.statement: Gonococcal prostatitis is a common complication of gonorrhea.
items.explanation: Gonococcal prostatitis occurs rarely, if at all.

items.statement: Pharyngeal gonorrhea often coexists with genital infection.
items.explanation: Most cases of pharyngeal gonorrhea occur alongside genital infection.

items.statement: Gonococcal isolates from the rectum are generally less resistant to antimicrobial agents than those from other sites.
items.explanation: Gonococcal isolates from the rectum tend to be more resistant to antimicrobial agents.

items.statement: Gonococcal endocarditis is now a common complication of disseminated gonococcal infection.
items.explanation: Gonococcal endocarditis, although rare today, was more common in the preantibiotic era.

items.statement: Pharyngeal colonization is seen in a significant percentage of infants with gonococcal ophthalmia.
items.explanation: Pharyngeal colonization has been demonstrated in 35% of infants with gonococcal ophthalmia.

items.statement: Gonorrhea may increase an individual's risk for acquiring HIV.
items.explanation: Gonorrhea increases the risk of HIV acquisition due to higher numbers of susceptible cells.

items.statement: HIV is more commonly detected in endocervical secretions from women with ulcerative STIs.
items.explanation: HIV is more commonly detected in nonulcerative STIs due to higher numbers of susceptible cells.

items.statement: Gonococci can be grown from the transport systems used in non-culture-based assays.
items.explanation: A disadvantage of non-culture-based assays is that N. gonorrhoeae cannot be grown from the transport systems.

items.statement: Blood cultures are most likely to yield positive results within 48 hours of illness onset in disseminated gonococcal infection.
items.explanation: The probability of positive blood cultures decreases after 48 h of illness.

items.statement: Spectinomycin is available and used in the United States for treating uncomplicated gonococcal infections.
items.explanation: Spectinomycin is unavailable in the United States.

items.statement: Quinolone-containing regimens are recommended as first-line treatment for uncomplicated gonorrhea in the United States.
items.explanation: Quinolone-containing regimens are no longer recommended due to widespread resistance.

items.statement: A single dose of ceftriaxone combined with saline irrigation is used to manage ocular gonococcal infections in adults.
items.explanation: This regimen is recommended for managing ocular gonococcal infections.

items.statement: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents are contraindicated in the treatment of DGI.
items.explanation: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents may be used to alleviate pain and hasten clinical improvement.

items.assertion: Asymptomatic gonorrhea infections contribute to the sustained transmission of the disease.
items.reason: Individuals with asymptomatic infections may continue sexual activity, thereby transmitting the infection.
items.explanation: The presence of a large number of infected individuals who are asymptomatic or have minor symptoms contributes to ongoing STI transmission.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · Gonococcal Infections | DEFINITION | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Antibiotic resistance in N. gonorrhoeae is decreasing.
items.reason: The CDC has identified antibiotic-resistant N. gonorrhoeae as one of the three most urgent threats.
items.explanation: Antibiotic resistance in N. gonorrhoeae is increasing, not decreasing, which is why it's considered an urgent threat.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · Gonococcal Infections | DEFINITION | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: PorB.1A strains are often associated with disseminated gonococcal infection.
items.reason: PorB.1A strains bind to complement-inhibitory molecules, resulting in diminished inflammatory response.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why PorB.1A strains are associated with DGI.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.assertion: Opacity-associated protein (Opa) is the most abundant gonococcal surface protein.
items.reason: Opa contributes to inter-gonococcal adhesion and adherence to eukaryotic cells.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; porin is the most abundant gonococcal surface protein, not Opa.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 1)

items.assertion: Gonococcal LOS sialylation decreases nonopsonic Opa-mediated association with neutrophils.
items.reason: Sialylation at LOS sites inhibits the classic pathway of complement by reducing IgG binding.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.assertion: Rmp antibodies block the effect of bactericidal antibodies to porin and LOS.
items.reason: Rmp shows extensive interstrain antigenic variation.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but Rmp actually shows little interstrain antigenic variation.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 2)

items.assertion: Ceftriaxone remains highly effective as a single-dose therapy for gonorrhea.
items.reason: The MICs of ceftriaxone against certain strains may reach levels that are still exceeded in the blood, urethra, and cervix with standard dosing.
items.explanation: Ceftriaxone is effective because its MICs are exceeded by standard doses at infection sites.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 3)

items.assertion: Spectinomycin resistance in N. gonorrhoeae is widespread.
items.reason: Outbreaks caused by spectinomycin-resistant strains have been documented when it was used for primary treatment.
items.explanation: While outbreaks of resistance exist, spectinomycin is still recommended as an alternative in certain regions.
items.reference: Gonococcal Infections · PATHOGENESIS, IMMUNOLOGY, AND ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (part 3)

items.assertion: Symptomatic men with gonorrhea cease to be infectious after seeking treatment.
items.reason: Most symptomatic men seek treatment and stop being infectious.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Gonococcal infection in women can extend deep enough to produce dyspareunia.
items.reason: Gonococcal infection is limited to the cervical os and does not affect deeper structures.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as gonococcal infection can extend deep enough to produce dyspareunia.

items.assertion: Ocular gonorrhea in adults is often a result of autoinoculation from an infected genital site.
items.reason: The infecting strain's ability to elicit an inflammatory response can vary, leading to different clinical manifestations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.

items.assertion: Gonorrhea in pregnant women can lead to complications such as premature delivery.
items.reason: The risks of salpingitis and PID are highest during the first trimester.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.

items.assertion: Gonococcal arthritis disseminated infection is more common today than in the 1970s.
items.reason: The lower incidence of DGI at present is probably attributable to a decline in the prevalence of particular strains that are likely to disseminate.
items.explanation: Gonococcal arthritis disseminated infection is less common today than in the 1970s.

items.assertion: Complement deficiencies predispose to Neisseria bacteremia.
items.reason: Deficiencies of components involved in the assembly of the membrane attack complex (C5 through C9) predispose to Neisseria bacteremia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: NAATs are preferred for diagnosing gonorrhea in the rectum and pharynx of adult victims of suspected sexual abuse.
items.reason: NAATs have been evaluated by local laboratories and found to be superior for these sites.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.

items.assertion: Gonococci are frequently recovered from early joint effusions containing <20,000 leukocytes/μL.
items.reason: Synovial fluid is not likely to be contaminated with commensal bacteria.
items.explanation: A is false because gonococci are infrequently recovered from early joint effusions containing <20,000 leukocytes/μL.

items.assertion: Ceftriaxone is the most reliable agent for treating pharyngeal gonorrhea.
items.reason: Ceftriaxone has been recommended as the first-line regimen due to its effectiveness against current strains of N. gonorrhoeae.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ceftriaxone is reliable for pharyngeal infection.

items.assertion: Hospitalization is always required for patients with uncomplicated gonorrhea.
items.reason: Hospitalization ensures adherence to treatment and monitoring of symptoms.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; hospitalization is not always required, only in specific cases such as uncertain diagnosis or inability to adhere to outpatient treatment.
